atoms.semantic_claim: The main `reth-node-ethereum` crate gains a direct `reth-db` dependency instead of relying only on test-only access.

destinations.evidence: The ported crate still depends on `reth-db`, but only in `dev-dependencies`, matching the remaining uses in tests rather than runtime code.

atoms.equivalence_argument: Downstream added `reth-db` to normal dependencies, but both downstream and port only reference `reth_db` from tests/docs in this crate. The port narrows it to `dev-dependencies`, which is behaviorally sufficient for the current code layout.
atoms.notes: Section changed from runtime to dev-only dependency.

atoms.semantic_claim: The crate explicitly depends on `alloy-primitives` in its main dependency set for runtime code and tests.

destinations.evidence: The port keeps `alloy-primitives` as a stronger runtime dependency with `serde` enabled and also retains the plain workspace dependency in `dev-dependencies`.

atoms.equivalence_argument: The exact dependency line moved, but the port still exposes `alloy-primitives` to runtime code through a stronger dependency and to tests through the plain dev-dependency.
atoms.notes: Covered by a stronger runtime dep plus retained test dep.

atoms.semantic_claim: The ethereum node crate directly links Ritual async-transaction support as a first-class dependency.

destinations.evidence: The port workspace replaces the monolithic `ritual-async` crate with multiple async-focused crates.

destinations.snippet:
ritual-async-inspector.workspace = true
ritual-async-fees.workspace = true
ritual-async-registry.workspace = true
destinations.evidence: Async-specific functionality is now consumed from `reth-ethereum-payload` instead of from `reth-node-ethereum`.
The node crate still exposes Ritual async RPC surface, but no longer through a direct `ritual-async` dependency.

atoms.equivalence_argument: The Ritual async feature set was not carried over as a single dependency in `reth-node-ethereum`; it was decomposed into `ritual-async-*` crates and partially relocated to payload and RPC integration paths.
atoms.negative_test_candidate: Build `reth-node-ethereum` alone in the port and assert that async RPC still works without any direct `ritual-async` crate linkage; a failure would show the split crates did not fully replace the old dependency.
atoms.human_question: Confirm that splitting the old `ritual-async` dependency across payload and RPC crates fully preserves the downstream async-node behavior that used to hang off `reth-node-ethereum`.
atoms.notes: High risk because the dependency was split and relocated, not preserved one-for-one.

atoms.semantic_claim: The ethereum node crate directly links Ritual scheduling support.

destinations.evidence: The port workspace provides a renamed/split scheduling-oriented crate instead of the original `ritual-scheduling` dependency.
Scheduling-related behavior is consumed by payload construction in the port rather than by `reth-node-ethereum` directly.
The old dependency still exists in the downstream dependency repo, showing the v2.2 port did not preserve this exact manifest edge in `reth-node-ethereum`.

atoms.equivalence_argument: Scheduling logic appears to have moved out of `reth-node-ethereum` and into a new scheduled-block-building path plus Ritual nodebuilder integration.
atoms.negative_test_candidate: Exercise scheduled block-building on the port; if scheduling hooks are absent unless `ritual-scheduling` is reintroduced directly into `reth-node-ethereum`, this mapping is insufficient.
atoms.human_question: Confirm that scheduled-block-building plus nodebuilder integration fully replaces the old direct `ritual-scheduling` dependency edge from `reth-node-ethereum`.
atoms.notes: High risk because the dependency name and owning crate changed.

atoms.semantic_claim: The ethereum node crate directly provides the SPC verification bridge through `ritual-spc-verification`.
atoms.negative_test_candidate: Attempt to compile a consumer against the port with `use reth_node_ethereum::SpcVerifierImpl;`; unresolved import or missing SPC verification wiring falsifies any claim that this dependency was preserved.
atoms.required_change: No code change in this v2 slice; behavior is intentionally inactive or out of scope.
atoms.notes: Old `reth-node-ethereum` SPC bridge dependency is intentionally not restored; the active nodebuilder binary compiles against the v2.2 tree without it, and live verification uses ritual-block-verification plus ritual-spc-signature.

atoms.semantic_claim: The crate exposes an `async-context` feature that forwards async execution support through `reth-evm-ethereum`.
atoms.negative_test_candidate: Run `cargo check -p reth-node-ethereum --features async-context` in the port; if it fails because the feature is gone, only the moved Ritual-node path can provide equivalent async-context behavior.
atoms.notes: High risk because this is a public Cargo feature API change. Guideline remediation: marked missing because The public `reth-node-ethereum/async-context` feature no longer exists in the port. Treat as missing unless a human explicitly approves the public API removal. Guideline remediation: accepted as intentionally absent because The user explicitly approved dropping the public `reth-node-ethereum/async-context` Cargo feature as an API-surface detail, provided the Reth chain preserves the same async-context runtime behavior elsewhere.
atoms.intentional_absence_rationale: The user explicitly approved dropping the public `reth-node-ethereum/async-context` Cargo feature as an API-surface detail, provided the Reth chain preserves the same async-context runtime behavior elsewhere.

summary: Atoms in this file have destination evidence or intentional scope classification after the feature-ordered pass.
notes: Most atom mappings are non-exact manifest/API reshapes; `ritual-spc-verification` is the main unresolved gap.
